moveAnnotations.annotation: The Grob Opening is an offbeat, aggressive system where we immediately challenge Black's control of the center by advancing our kingside pawns. By playing g4, we're preparing to fianchetto our bishop on g2—a long-range piece that will eye the center and queenside—while also gaining space on the flank. This move is unconventional and relies on surprise value and dynamic play rather than classical opening theory, making it popular with creative players who want to avoid main-line battles.

Black immediately strikes at the center with d5, the principled response to Grob's flank strategy. By playing in the middle rather than matching your g4 with a symmetrical g6, Black seizes space and prevents your light-squared bishop from becoming too dominant; this is why the Grob is considered "declined" when Black plays this central break instead of accepting g4 as a gambit.

We complete the fianchetto setup, placing our bishop on the long diagonal (a1-h8) where it exerts pressure on Black's center and queenside. The bishop on g2 is a hallmark of the Grob—it supports central ideas and controls key squares like d5, giving us some compensation for our unusual opening. Even though we've only played two moves, this bishop is already influencing the game's direction.

Black reinforces the d5 pawn and prepares to support the center with moves like Bf5 or Nf6, while also keeping options open for a queenside expansion. The move c6 is flexible and solid—it prevents our pieces from settling on d5 and gives Black a sturdy pawn structure to build from. Notice Black isn't playing ultra-aggressively either, suggesting both sides are taking a measured approach in this unconventional opening.
